Eduarda, I read your draft to the client. The structure is clear, but the salutation and the tone feel too direct.
You are right. I will acknowledge their last message first, then soften the request.
Good. How will you ask for the figures?
Instead of send the numbers, I will write I would appreciate the figures by Friday, if possible.
Much better. Keep the body concise, and do not forget the deck.
I will attach the deck and name it in the message, so the recipient does not miss it.
Perfect. And copy me on the reply. How will you close it?
With a warm sign-off: Best regards. Then I will forward you the final version.
Thank you for your message; I am writing to confirm the timeline.
Could you please send the figures by Friday?
I would appreciate the report by end of day.
Please find attached the updated deck.
I look forward to your prompt reply. Best regards.

Hi Eduarda, quick note before you send that draft. I read the email to the client and the structure is good, but the tone is a little too direct. Please soften the opening line. Instead of give me the numbers by Friday, write I would appreciate the figures by Friday if possible. Also acknowledge their last message before you ask for anything, and keep the body concise, just three short paragraphs. Add the deck as an attachment, and copy me on the reply. Sign off with a warm regards rather than thanks. Send it to the recipient by end of day.

Hi team, here is a reminder on email tone before the deal heats up. Every message to a client should follow the same structure. Open by acknowledging what they wrote. State your purpose in one clear line, I am writing to confirm the timeline. Keep the body concise and easy to scan. If you attach a file, name it in the message so the recipient does not miss it. End with a polite sign off, and reply promptly, ideally within a few hours. A well written email protects the relationship as much as the deal itself.

Eduarda opened the email by acknowledging the client's last message. She stated her purpose in one clear line. She kept the body concise and added the report as an attachment. She copied her director on the reply. She signed off politely and sent it to the recipient.
